name: Horus Extended
description: Horus, in his extended form, encompasses a vast and multifaceted representation of kingship, protection, and divine power within the Egyptian pantheon. Born from the union of Osiris and Isis, Horus is primarily known as the falcon-headed god, embodying the living pharaoh. His origins are rooted in the myth of his father's murder by Set, the god of chaos and disorder. Isis, through her magical abilities, resurrected Osiris long enough to conceive Horus. Hidden away in the Delta marshes, Horus was raised in secrecy, preparing for his eventual challenge to Set for the throne of Egypt. The ensuing battles between Horus and Set are legendary, often depicted as a series of trials and tribulations that tested Horus's strength, cunning, and worthiness to rule. These conflicts resulted in Horus losing his eye, which was later restored by Thoth, becoming the Eye of Horus, a powerful symbol of protection, healing, and royal power. Horus's reign is associated with justice, order, and the prosperity of Egypt. He is seen as the protector of the pharaoh, ensuring the stability and well-being of the kingdom. His influence extends to the afterlife, where he guides the deceased and assists in their transition. The extended understanding of Horus also incorporates his various forms and aspects, including Harsiesis (Horus the Child), Haroeris (Horus the Elder), and Harmakhis (Horus in the Horizon), each representing different stages of his life and power. His association with the sun further solidifies his role as a symbol of light, hope, and rebirth. Ultimately, Horus represents the triumph of good over evil, order over chaos, and the enduring power of kingship and divine authority in ancient Egyptian belief.
subtitle: Falcon-headed God of Kingship and Protection

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Horus
keyMyths.description: Isis conceives Horus after Osiris's death and hides him in the Delta marshes to protect him from Set.

keyMyths.title: The Battles with Set
keyMyths.description: Horus battles Set for the throne of Egypt, undergoing numerous trials and losing his eye in the process.
keyMyths.source: Book of the Dead, Edfu Texts

keyMyths.title: The Healing of the Eye of Horus
keyMyths.description: Thoth restores Horus's eye, which becomes a powerful symbol of protection and healing.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: Erik Hornung

sources.description: Explores the complex nature of divinity in ancient Egypt.
